thesis: Virgin Galactic is a pre-revenue commercial spaceflight company with a binary catalyst: the Delta-class vehicle program. First ship enters ground tests in April 2026, flight tests in Q3 2026, and commercial service is targeted for Q4 2026. If successful, the company guides $450M annual revenue and $100M adjusted EBITDA at fleet scale. However, FY2025 revenue was only $1.54M against a $279M net loss and $438M negative free cash flow. Cash of $338M provides roughly 3-4 quarters of runway at current burn rates. The stock has more than doubled from lows and RSI is overbought at 78+. This is a pure speculative call option on program execution.

drivers: Delta-class launches on schedule in Q4 2026 with no technical issues
Second ship enters service in early 2027, enabling $450M annual revenue target
Gross margins expand rapidly as flight cadence increases
Virgin Galactic captures first-mover advantage in high-frequency suborbital tourism

risks: Delta-class encounters significant technical delays or failure during flight test
Cash runs critically low by Q4 2026; distressed financing or strategic review
Suborbital tourism demand fails to materialize at $450K price point

base_case.assumptions: First Delta-class flight slips to early 2027; one ship operational through most of 2027. Revenue remains minimal ($50–100M) through 2027. Additional capital raise dilutes existing shareholders 15–20%. Stock trades sideways as investors wait for proof of commercial viability.

catalysts.event: Flight test phase begins for first Delta-class SpaceShip
Target for first commercial Delta-class flight
Second Delta-class ship expected to enter service

key_risks: Delta-class program delays or technical failures
Cash runway of only ~3-4 quarters at current burn rates
High likelihood of additional dilutive capital raises
Competition from Blue Origin (New Shepard) and SpaceX
Luxury discretionary spending vulnerability in recessions
Overbought technical conditions (RSI 78+) suggest pullback risk
